title: If the Ganges Itself Becomes Impure, Where Can One Go to Bathe?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's legal chronology, attributed allegations, quoted judicial observations, political directness, rhetorical questions, irony, figures, dates, and paragraph order. Intentional English legal passages are retained. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which only clear OCR character, spacing, line-break, and non-text scan errors are repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: When the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa and others was first tried in Tamil Nadu, the AIADMK was the ruling party. Seeing that the trial was not proceeding properly, our General Secretary, the Professor, filed a case seeking its transfer to another State. On 18 November 2003, a Supreme Court Bench comprising Justices S.N. Variava and H.K. Sema ordered that the trial be transferred to Karnataka.

The Supreme Court judges said in that case: “It does appear that the new public prosecutor is hand in glove with the accused thereby creating a reasonable apprehension of likelihood of failure of justice in the minds of the public at large. There is strong indication that the process of justice is being subverted.” In other words, it was clearly apparent that the new Public Prosecutor was acting in concert with the accused, Jayalalithaa's group. This created in the public mind a reasonable apprehension that justice would not be done. It was evident that justice was being diverted from its course. When questions were to be put under Section 313, the Public Prosecutor did not even oppose the frivolous petition Jayalalithaa filed to avoid appearing personally before the court, and merely watched. That was deplorable. “The conduct of the Public Prosecutor in not opposing such a frivolous application has to be deprecated.”

The Supreme Court judges expressed their strong objection to the trial court allowing Jayalalithaa to dispense with her personal appearance: “Be you ever so high, the law is above you. The grounds cited by her in the application were not at all mitigating circumstances to have granted dispensation of personal appearance. To say the least, that was a ploy adopted to circumvent the due process of law.”

The judges further said: “In our view, the petitioner has raised many justifiable and reasonable apprehensions of miscarriage of justice and likelihood of bias, which would require our interference.” That is, the petitioner, the Professor, had raised reasonable and substantial apprehensions that justice might miscarry and proceed with bias, making the Court's intervention necessary. The Supreme Court itself said so. The other important directions in that judgment included the following. Since the Court found sufficient prima facie material in the DMK's case, there was a possibility that justice would not be secured if the case were tried in Tamil Nadu. It was therefore a fit case for transfer.
The Chief Justice of the Karnataka High Court, in consultation with the Karnataka Government, was to establish a Special Court in Bengaluru under the Prevention of Corruption Act within six weeks.
A senior advocate experienced in criminal cases was to be appointed Public Prosecutor, together with a junior advocate.

The Public Prosecutor appointed by the Chief Justice was to recall and re-examine the prosecution witnesses who had turned hostile, and was also instructed to initiate separate criminal proceedings against witnesses who had deliberately changed their testimony.
If witnesses faced threats, the Karnataka Government was to provide them adequate police protection.
All the accused were to appear without fail when questioned, and the case was to be heard on a daily basis.
The Tamil Nadu Government was immediately to send all documents connected with the case to the Special Court.

The Supreme Court's order that the case be tried expeditiously and from day to day was issued on 18 November 2003. The AIADMK was then in power in Tamil Nadu. Do you know when the Chennai Special Court issued the formal order transferring the case to Bengaluru in compliance with that Supreme Court direction?
On 10 September 2004. In other words, the order was issued only ten months later.

Ten years have passed since then, and the case is still unfinished. The reason is nothing other than the accused repeatedly obtaining adjournments on various pretexts, with the intention of delaying the trial. If they were in the right, why would they need to drag it out by seeking adjournments without limit? Only one carrying a burden in one's lap fears the road!

When the trial was transferred to Bengaluru, the case was already nearing completion. A judgment was expected within a few months. Yet because of the accused's skill in securing adjournments, it has been dragged out for ten years.

A Public Prosecutor is supposed to argue against the accused. But the DMK charged that the present Public Prosecutor, Bhavani Singh, was arguing in support of the accused instead. The Karnataka Government accepted that charge and removed him as Public Prosecutor. Yet Jayalalithaa's side, the accused, went all the way to the Supreme Court and argued that Bhavani Singh, who was supposed to argue against them, must continue as Public Prosecutor, and that Judge Balakrishna, who was retiring, should continue hearing the case. Those who believe in the rule of law were astonished and shocked by this strange spectacle. Retired Judge Balakrishna did not come forward to continue the trial, and the case is now being heard by the upright Judge John Michael D'Cunha.

Even though the Karnataka Government itself had replaced Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh, the Supreme Court decided that he could continue to conduct the case for the State. That decision was made by the same Supreme Court judge, Justice Chauhan, who has now delivered the present order.

Although many examples were placed before the Supreme Court of the ways in which Bhavani Singh had acted in support of Jayalalithaa's side and to the detriment of the prosecution, Jayalalithaa's request was ultimately accepted. He was permitted to remain Public Prosecutor and continues in that post.

Only two months ago, the accused insisted that this very Public Prosecutor must continue. Now the same Bhavani Singh says that he is unwell, cannot argue, and wants the case adjourned. Can everyone understand the truth concealed within this, or not?

At the hearing on 27 January 2014, lawyers for the accused filed petitions seeking exemption from personal appearance. They said Jayalalithaa had official duties as Chief Minister, Sasikala had eye pain, Sudhakaran had joint pain, and Ilavarasi had diabetes, and therefore they could not attend court. Although our advocate Thamaraichelvan objected, the opposing counsel who ought to have objected, namely the Public Prosecutor, raised no objection at all. Because Bhavani Singh did not object, the judge accepted the request. Even then, the judge said: “No medical certificates have been enclosed before the court. Nevertheless, I accept these petitions for this occasion alone.”

Jayalalithaa's lawyer next filed a new petition asking the court to order the return of articles seized during searches of their clients' homes that were unrelated to the case. When the case was nearing completion, why did the accused, who had remained silent all these years, suddenly need to make such a request? Was the object to delay the case?

When the judge asked the Public Prosecutor to file objections to that petition, Bhavani Singh sought two weeks. The judge merely asked whether there was any objection to returning the seized articles, yet the Public Prosecutor wanted two weeks and asked that the trial be adjourned for that period. Is the attempt to secure an adjournment on one pretext or another not evident? Why did he need so much time? It was the Public Prosecutor himself who asked for it. With whom did he need all that time to consult?

The judge refused and directed him to submit his objection, orally or in writing, by 31 January. The Public Prosecutor then sought at least a week, saying he needed to consult Tamil Nadu's Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption officials. The judge rejected that request too.

Expressing displeasure at the prosecution's request for time, the judge asked a vital question: “Is this a criminal court or an adjournment court?” Special Court Judge D'Cunha further observed: “This trial has been going on for fifteen years. There have been more days of adjournment than days of hearing. The purpose of appointing a Special Court and a special judge was to conduct the trial daily and complete it quickly. That purpose has not been fully achieved. At least from now on, the trial must proceed without interruption.”

At the hearing on 20 January, Jayalalithaa's lawyers filed a petition seeking all particulars of movable property brought from Chennai to Bengaluru and the list of gift articles. The judge dismissed it.

To facilitate speedy completion, the judge also announced that final arguments would begin on 3 February, that hearings would be held daily in accordance with the Supreme Court's order, that the Public Prosecutor would argue first and counsel for the accused afterwards, and that the start of final arguments could under no circumstances be delayed.

Even after more than fifteen years of planned delay, various devices and tactics continued to be employed to postpone the case further. One such device was a petition filed by the Public Prosecutor only days earlier. He asked the judge to have 1,116 kilograms of silver articles belonging to Jayalalithaa, said to have been entrusted to a man named Bhaskaran in Chennai, brought to the Bengaluru court. Why did the Public Prosecutor need to file such a petition when the case was about to end? The petition was filed, in my view, to delay the proceedings. But a petition submitted on behalf of the Professor informed the court that Bhaskaran had already died.
It was while dismissing this petition that the Special Judge delivered his order on 28 February 2014. In that order he stated:

“This case has been proceeding in Bengaluru for more than nine years. When Judge B.A. Mallikarjunaiah heard it, the accused appeared in person and gave their statements under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. When Judge Balakrishna heard it, examination of witnesses was completed. Counsel for both the prosecution and the accused completed their arguments. On 14 August 2013 itself, Judge Balakrishna recorded that every matter connected with the case had been completed. The case has now reached its final stage. For the past thirteen years, the prosecution took no steps to obtain these silver articles belonging to Jayalalithaa's side from Bhaskaran and produce them before the court.

“When, on a petition filed by a third party in the case, the General Secretary of the DMK, the gold and silver ornaments in Chennai were brought to Bengaluru, why did Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh not file a petition seeking these silver articles from Bhaskaran?
“Bhavani Singh has served as Public Prosecutor since February 2013. During that period he filed no such petition.

“Bhavani Singh says he was appointed Public Prosecutor in this case and had argued for three days. Did he not know that the first accused's silver articles were in Chennai? His claim that he learned of them only when reading the documents for final arguments is neither truthful nor credible. He knew that Bhaskaran, who had taken the silver articles, was no longer alive. Even after Bhaskaran's death was established before the court with a death certificate, the Public Prosecutor refused to accept it, persisted in his position, and continued pressing the petition. That is not legally acceptable.

“This gives rise to the view that Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh acted with the intention of delaying the case. All the silver articles in the first accused's home had been valued, and only their monetary value was included. The case records contain the relevant details. When the trial is ending, there is no need to waste time bringing the silver articles here. That argument too is unsound. The Public Prosecutor's petition seeking their production therefore has no basis. I regard it as unnecessary and dismiss it.”

If this is what the Special Judge recorded in his order on Bhavani Singh's petition, do we need any further example to understand how the Public Prosecutor has been acting in favour of the accused in this case?

And it was precisely to keep this lawyer, Bhavani Singh, as Public Prosecutor that Jayalalithaa's side went all the way to the Supreme Court. Do you now understand the true meaning and motive behind their case, or not? In the disproportionate-assets case, Jayalalithaa has two lawyers: one is Bhavani Singh, paid by the Government, who disregards the Government's own prosecution, fails in the duty he must perform, and argues in support of the accused Jayalalithaa; the other is her own lawyer, B. Kumar, paid by Jayalalithaa to argue for her. Even if Tamil Nadu's newspapers try to conceal this shameful truth, something never before seen or heard in judicial history, can it remain unknown to the people?

Judge John Michael D'Cunha announced that final arguments would begin on 7 March 2014. But the Public Prosecutor did not appear in court that day. The case was adjourned to the tenth. Bhavani Singh failed to appear on that day too. The matter next came up on 14 March, when the judge ordered final arguments to begin. Bhavani Singh appeared, said that he was unwell, sought ten days' time in continuation of his efforts to delay the trial, and produced medical certificates. Dissatisfied with those certificates, the judge ordered Bhavani Singh, who had attended court but refused to argue, to pay a fine equal to one day's fee, Rs 65,000. On the fifteenth, Bhavani Singh again failed to appear, without notice or explanation. His assistant Murugesh said he knew nothing about the Public Prosecutor's absence. The judge therefore imposed a further fine of Rs 65,000, his fee for that day.

The lawyers appearing for Jayalalithaa said they could begin their arguments only after the Public Prosecutor completed his. The judge then firmly ordered the Public Prosecutor to begin his arguments on 21 March 2014.

Meanwhile, Bhavani Singh petitioned the Karnataka High Court to cancel the fine imposed by the Special Court for his non-appearance. Karnataka High Court Justice Sathyanarayana Rao dismissed the petition. Bhavani Singh therefore appeared before the Special Court on 21 March and began his final arguments, recounting what the witnesses in the case had testified. Only now is the world coming to know what the witnesses said about Jayalalithaa accumulating assets worth many crores of rupees.

I spoke during my election tour about the truths those witnesses had stated concerning this disproportionate-assets case, and a few newspapers began publishing them. That was enough! Can those who have done wrong bear it? They are struggling in every possible way to devise some means of securing another adjournment and delaying the trial.

One such course began after the Public Prosecutor appointed by the Government to argue against Jayalalithaa was accused of neglecting his duty and was removed by the Karnataka Government itself. Jayalalithaa's side went to the Supreme Court insisting that this same Bhavani Singh must continue. Justice Chauhan then ordered that he could do so. When that Public Prosecutor repeatedly failed to appear before the Special Court, the Special Judge fined him.

Bhavani Singh appealed to the Karnataka High Court, where his petition was dismissed. Now he has taken the matter to the Supreme Court. You must judge for yourselves all the mysteries that may lie behind this.
Why do the accused want a particular lawyer appointed by the Government to argue against them to remain in the case?
Why does that particular lawyer go all the way to the Supreme Court and ask that this case be stayed for another three weeks?
If Bhavani Singh is truly unwell, could he not resign from the post? Or could the trial not proceed through his assistant? Once his health improves, could he not return and continue the proceedings?
After the Supreme Court ordered in 2003 that the case be completed quickly and heard daily, why must the Supreme Court itself now act contrary to that order and stay the trial for three weeks?

These three weeks fall at a crucial time. The parliamentary general election is to be held during that period. If the case proceeds and judgment is delivered before then, perhaps it could affect the accused. Has this “adjournment of the case” therefore occurred in a manner that causes impartial observers to suspect precisely that? What is the secret behind it?

If an entire trial can be postponed for three weeks because one lawyer is unwell, what will be the result if every lawyer in the country obtains a medical certificate to secure an adjournment and asks for a stay of three weeks or three months?

The Supreme Court Bench that made this order comprised Justices B.S. Chauhan and J. Chelameswar. We cannot forget that Justice B.S. Chauhan is due to retire in a few days. He is also the judge who, only weeks ago, ordered that Bhavani Singh could continue as Public Prosecutor in this case. Bhavani Singh's principal request concerned the fine. But if the Supreme Court stays the Special Court trial itself for three weeks, can you see the remarkable generosity with which the Supreme Court has acted? That is why, speaking at Salem, I recalled Kodumudi Kokilam K.B. Sundarambal's song in the film *Poompuhar*: “Justice, are you still alive? Or did you too perish in that killing field?”

How many years, then, has this disproportionate-assets case continued, dragged out by adjournment upon adjournment in a manner without precedent in judicial history? Though the case was filed over the accumulation of assets worth about Rs 65 crore, the value of those assets has now grown to nearly Rs 5,000 crore. More particularly, one can understand the planned efforts being made to choke and bend justice in this case. Lawyers across India are watching how a Public Prosecutor, who should argue for honesty and justice on behalf of the State, acts in sympathy with the accused.

There are 259 witnesses in all. Bhavani Singh has finished reading the testimony of 209. Only fifty remain. Barely one day's work is left. It is at this stage that the Supreme Court has stayed the trial for three weeks. The Special Court's fine upon Bhavani Singh for two days was Rs 1.20 lakh. Do you know how much he has spent to avoid paying it? Lawyers appearing in the Supreme Court charge at least Rs 10 lakh for a case. Nagendra Rai, who argued there for Bhavani Singh, is a senior advocate and would have been paid Rs 10 lakh. Apart from that, filing a petition in the Supreme Court costs Rs 2 lakh. Would anyone spend Rs 12 lakh to save Rs 1.20 lakh? Judging by Bhavani Singh's finances as a Public Prosecutor, he does not appear wealthy enough to pay Rs 12 lakh. Is it clear, or not, that someone used him as a pawn, spent money for him in the Supreme Court, and engaged a senior advocate? What benefit did those behind him expect? Who believes that Bhavani Singh's continuation and his arguments will serve their interests? The three-week adjournment enables them to prevent judgment before the parliamentary election. If they fear that a judgment might affect the election result, does that not mean they carry a burden in their lap?

Does the three-week adjournment not mean that they want judgment postponed until after the election? I am not alone in saying this. Economist and legal expert Dr K. Sridhar said: “Ordinarily it is the accused who seek adjournments. In this case, the prosecution seeking an adjournment is not proper procedure. One thing is clear: Bhavani Singh did not approach the Supreme Court at his own expense.” Senior advocate Suresh Nayak similarly said: “Legal experts are watching how the person arguing for the prosecution in this case, Bhavani Singh, is functioning. There is no legal expert or politician who has failed to observe the course of a case that has challenged the legal system for the past seventeen years. Bhavani Singh had no need to go all the way to the Supreme Court against the Special Court's action in fining him. Nor is he in a position to spend several lakhs to cancel a small fine. What is clear is that certain political forces are using him for their own interests.”

Somehow, they have secured a Supreme Court stay preventing the trial from proceeding for three weeks. The people understand very well that neither the Public Prosecutor's health nor the fine imposed on him is the reason for that stay. They also understand what the reason is. Tamil Nadu's voters are capable of understanding who is trying to divert the case through all these measures and why. They will think carefully, and they will decide wisely!

Chief Justice of India Sathasivam is due to retire shortly. Justice Chauhan, who postponed the disproportionate-assets trial for three weeks, is also due to retire soon. Justice Lodha, who is to become Chief Justice, delivered an important judgment on 10 March. He ruled: “In cases against serving Members of Parliament and Members of Legislative Assemblies, courts must complete the trial within one year of the case being instituted. If a court cannot complete it within a year, it must explain the reason to the Chief Justice of the High Court concerned. Cases against MPs and MLAs must be heard daily so that they may be completed quickly. Because such cases remain pending for many years, many persons facing serious charges continue to serve as members of Parliament and the State legislatures.”

When we consider that judgment, the provision in the Prevention of Corruption Act that trials under the Act must not be stayed, and the Supreme Court's contrary action in staying this trial for three weeks, I am reminded of a phrase Pasumpon Muthuramalinga Thevar often used: “If a woman becomes impure, she may bathe in the Ganges. If the Ganges itself becomes impure, where can she go to bathe?”
By doing all this, judgments may be delayed. But they certainly cannot alter the judgment and escape. A just and honest judgment must come soon! Then the town and the world will know the truth!
With affection,
M.K.
